elements.sent-2411-001: Sequential quantum information processing may lie in the peaceful coexistence of no-go theorems on quantum operations, such as the no-cloning theorem, the monogamy of correlations, and the no-signalling principle.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this work, we investigate a sequential scenario of quantum state discrimination with maximum confidence, called maximum-confidence discrimination, which generalizes other strategies including minimum-error and unambiguous state discrimination.
elements.sent-2411-003: We show that sequential state discrimination with equally high confidence can be realized only when positive-operator-valued measure elements for a maximum-confidence measurement are linearly independent; otherwise, a party will have strictly less confidence in measurement outcomes than the previous one.
elements.sent-2411-004: We establish a tradeoff between the disturbance of states and information gain in sequential state discrimination, namely, that the less a party learn in state discrimination in terms of a guessing probability, the more parties can participate in the sequential scenario.
elements.sent-2411-005: Fundamental principles of quantum information processing contain the no-go theorems that non-orthogonal quantum states cannot be perfectly copied \cite{Wootters:1982aa, RevModPhys.77.1225 nor discriminated \cite{Helstrom1969, Bergou2004, Barnett09, Bae2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, being closely connected, quantum correlations, such as entanglement and nonlocal correlations, are monogamous \cite{PhysRevA.61.052306, PhysRevLett.96.220503, PhysRevA.73.012112 , restricting quantum information processing across multiple parties.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Peaceful coexistence of the no-go results can be observed in a sequential quatnum information task of multiple parties that apply non-destructive quantum operations, particularly weak measurements, by which the parties can sequentially extract nonlocal correlations \cite{PhysRevLett.114.250401 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: How weak a measurement is determines the number of parties in the sequential scenario \cite{Colbeck2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Sequential quantum state discrimination for an ensemble of two pure states has been shown such that many parties can sequentially perform unambiguous discrimination \cite{Bergou2013 , where conclusive outcomes do not give an incorrect guess.}
elements.sent-2411-010: In other words, quantum channels are constructed between parties such that each party knows which states are to appear, thus can choose a measurement for unambiguous discrimination, and then passes a resulting state to the next party, which can also realize unambiguous discrimination.
elements.sent-2411-011: In this case, it is not weak measurements that make a quantum protocol sequential across parties; it is non-optimal discrimination where none of the parties attempt to minimize the probability of inconclusive outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-012: Apart from the case of two pure states, little is known so far.
elements.sent-2411-013: In this work, we establish the framework for sequential quantum state discrimination with a maximum-confidence (MC) measurement \cite{Croke2006 , which generalizes other strategies, such as unambiguous and minimum-error state discrimination.}
elements.sent-2411-014: We present the construction of quantum channels between parties so that multiple parties can sequentially realize an MC measurement.
elements.sent-2411-015: Our findings show that all parties can achieve equally high confidence in the MC discrimination outcomes only when the positive operator-valued measurement (POVM) elements corresponding to the conclusive outcomes of the MC measurement are linearly independent.
elements.sent-2411-016: Otherwise, sequential parties cannot maintain confidence in measurement outcomes; a party will have strictly less confidence than the previous ones.
elements.sent-2411-017: This work is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-018: We begin with sequential MC discrimination for two mixed states to clarify the sequential scenario and elucidate its structure.
elements.sent-2411-019: We establish the tradeoff between information gain in terms of guessing and state disturbance in the sequential scenario, from which we find that the strength of measurements determines the number of parties that can participate in the sequential scenario, similar to the tradeoff in sequential violations of Bell inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-020: We present the necessary and sufficient conditions for realizing sequential MC discrimination with equally high confidence.
elements.sent-2411-021: We also investigate sequential MC discrimination for trine qubit states that are linearly dependent.
elements.sent-2411-022: The strength of weak measurements can determine how many parties can participate in the sequential state discrimination.
elements.sent-2411-023: Before starting sequential state discrimination, we briefly review a quantum MC measurement \cite{Croke2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: For an ensemble $\{q_{\x ,\rho_{\x}\}_{\x=1 ^n$, describing a state $\rho_{\x}$ given with a probability $q_{\x}$, an MC measurement provides the highest probability of making a correct guess about state preparation (P) once an outcome occurs in a measurement (M).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Namely, it maximizes a conditional probability $\mathrm{Prob _{P|M (\x|\x)$, defined as confidence $C_{\x}$ on an outcome $\x$ via Baye's rule as follows, \bea C_{\x := \max_{M \frac{q_{\x \mathrm{Prob _{M|P (\x|\x) {\mathrm{Prob _{ M ( \x) = \max_{M_{\x \frac{ q_{\x \tr[\rho_{\x M_{\x ] { \tr[\rho M_{\x ] \eea where $M_{\x}$ denotes a POVM element.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: An MC measurement with confidence $C_{\x =1$ realizes unambiguous discrimination.}
elements.sent-2411-027: It corresponds to minimum-error discrimination when confidence in all inconclusive outcomes is maximized on average and no inconclusive outcome occurs \cite{Barnett09 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: One can find that a rank-one POVM element achieves maximization in Eq. (\ref{eq:conf1 ); thus, all conclusive outcomes of an MC measurement are described by rank-one POVM elements \cite{Flatt2022, Lee2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Let us begin sequential discrimination with an ensemble of two quantum states with equal {\it a priori probabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The instance with two states clarifies and elucidates the sequential scenario and the structure.
elements.sent-2411-031: We consider discrimination between two states, \bea \rho_{\x = p \ketbra{\psi_{\x +\frac{1-p {2 \I, ~\mathrm{where \ket{\psi_{\x = \cos\frac{\theta {2}\ket{0 - (-1)^{\x \sin\frac{\theta {2}\ket{1 .\nonumber \eea for $p\in (0,1]$ and $\x=1,2$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-083: We summarize the result as follows.. {\it Proposition.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Sequential MC measurements with equally high confidence can be realized if and only if POVM elements are linearly independent.
elements.sent-2411-085: Otherwise, when POVM elements of an MC measurement are linearly dependent, one may exploit a weak measurement to maintain confidence as high as possible in the sequential scenario.
elements.sent-2411-086: Let $\{ M_{\x}\}_{\x=1 ^n$ denote POVM elements for conclusive outcomes of an MC measurement and $M_0$ for inconclusive ones.}

elements.sent-2411-098: The derivaion is detailed in Supplemental Material.
elements.sent-2411-099: Thus, parties in the sequential scenario can choose rates of inconclusive rate to weaken the measurement strength and attempt to maintain confidence as high as possible.
elements.sent-2411-100: In conclusion, we have established a sequential scenario with MC measurements, a unifying framework of state discrimination including minimum-error and unambiguous state discrimination.
elements.sent-2411-101: We have shown the structure of sequential state discrimination by identifying POVM elements for MC measurements and characterized the tradeoff between information gain and state disturbance in the sequential scenario.
elements.sent-2411-102: We have presented the necessary and sufficient condition for sequential MC discrimination: sequential MC discrimination can be realized if, and only if, POVM elements are linearly independent.
elements.sent-2411-103: Our findings deepen the understanding of quantum information processing over trusted and cooperative parties.
elements.sent-2411-104: It would be interesting to apply our results to practical tasks such as sequential randomness \cite{Carceller2022 and quantum information tasks across multiple parties.}

elements.sent-2411-129: For convenience, we consider $n+1$ Kraus operators so that $n$ POVM elements for conclusive outcomes are linearly independent, \bea K_{\x = \sqrt{c_x \ket{\phi_x \bra{ \widetilde{\varphi _{\x ,~~\mathrm{for ~ \x=1,...,n \nonumber \mathrm{and ~~K_{0 = \sum_{\x=1 ^n \sqrt{a_{\x \ket{\phi_{\x \bra{ \widetilde{\varphi _{\x .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Our findings show that all parties can achieve equally high confidence in the MC discrimination outcomes only when the positive operator-valued measure (POVM) elements corresponding to the conclusive outcomes of the MC measurement are linearly independent.
elements.sent-2411-131: In fact, considering all of the conclusive and inconclusive outcomes, one can find that a quantum channel does not increase the distinguishability of quantum states on average \cite{PhysRevLett.103.210401, RevModPhys.88.021002 , \bea \| \rho_1 - \rho_2 \|_1 \geq \| \mathcal{E (\rho_1 - \rho_2) \|_1, \eea for states $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: Since $\eta_0<1$, POVM elements in sequential MC measurements become less distinguishable, meaning states in an ensemble are also less distinguishable on average, see Eq. (\ref{eq:cont ), since \bea \sqrt{1- | \langle v_1 | v_2 \rangle|^2 = \frac{1 {2}\| |v_1 \rangle \langle v_1 | -|v_2 \rangle \langle v_2| \|_1 \nonumber \eea for two states $|v_1\rangle$ and $|v_2\rangle$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: We have presented the necessary and sufficient condition for sequential MC discrimination: sequential MC discrimination can be realized with equally high confidence in measurement outcomes if, and only if, POVM elements are linearly independent.
